fr:title.text: When is the partial map classifier a Sierpiński cone?

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: When is the partial map classifier a Sierpiński cone?

fr:meta.name: venue

fr:link.title: LICS ’25: 40th Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

fr:meta.fr:link: LICS ’25: 40th Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

fr:mainmatter.html:p: We study the relationship between partial map classifiers, Sierpiński cones, and axioms for synthetic higher categories and domains within univalent foundations. In particular, we show that synthetic ∞-categories are closed under partial map classifiers assuming Phoa's principle, and we isolate a new reflective subuniverse of types within which the Sierpiński cone (a lax colimit) can be computed as a partial map classifier by strengthening the Segal condition.

fr:title.text: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Homotopy Type Theory: univalent foundations of mathematics

fr:meta.name: venue

fr:link.title: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

fr:meta.fr:link: IAS Special Year on Univalent Foundations

fr:frontmatter.fr:meta: @book{hottbook, author = {{Univalent Foundations Program}, The}, address = {Institute for Advanced Study}, publisher = {\url{https://homotopytypetheory.org/book}}, year = {2013}, title = {Homotopy Type Theory: Univalent Foundations of Mathematics}, }

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Homotopy type theory is a new branch of mathematics that combines aspects of several different fields in a surprising way. It is based on a recently discovered connection between homotopy theory and type theory. It touches on topics as seemingly distant as the homotopy groups of spheres, the algorithms for type checking, and the definition of weak \infty -groupoids. Homotopy type theory offers a new “univalent” foundation of mathematics, in which a central role is played by Voevodsky’s univalence axiom and higher inductive types. The present book is intended as a first systematic exposition of the basics of univalent foundations, and a collection of examples of this new style of reasoning — but without requiring the reader to know or learn any formal logic, or to use any computer proof assistant. We believe that univalent foundations will eventually become a viable alternative to set theory as the “implicit foundation” for the unformalized mathematics done by most mathematicians.

fr:title.text: LICS ’25: 40th Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: LICS ’25: 40th Annual ACM/IEEE Symposium on Logic in Computer Science

fr:mainmatter.html:p: The LICS Symposium is an annual international forum on theoretical and practical topics in computer science that relate to logic, broadly construed.
